candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made into a non-question-begging formal theorem by specifying a precise class of systems, semantics, and 'correctness conditions' under which an incompleteness-style limitation actually follows? The main unresolved tension is whether the proposed bridge from Gödelian provability to self-representational knowledge is genuinely load-bearing or only analogical once 'interpretation,' 'aboutness,' and 'knowledge' are formalized.

What exactly distinguishes genuine self-knowledge from mere self-modeling or self-referential control in systems with internal fixed points? The open problem is to identify necessary and sufficient conditions for 'interpret those descriptions as about itself' and for 'causal/inferential efficacy' without smuggling in an external observer's semantics. Otherwise the conjecture risks collapsing either into triviality (many control systems count) or into an untestable metaphysical notion.

How strong and general is the claimed limit on 'total transparent self-knowledge'? An important unresolved question is whether the obstruction is universal for all sufficiently expressive self-modeling systems, or whether architectures can evade it by restricting the property class P, using probabilistic or hierarchical self-models, or relaxing stability/certifiability. This matters because the conjecture's explanatory power depends on whether the limitation is a deep structural necessity or only a feature of particular formal setups.
